title: The artificial swarm method
subtitle: Splitting a colony before it splits itself.
excerpt: A colony building swarm cells in late spring is ten days from leaving. The artificial swarm method uses that impulse to expand the apiary rather than lose the bees. The technique moves the flying bees onto the original site with a new queen cell and gives the queenright colony a fresh start on empty comb.
sourceNotes: BBKA guide to swarm control (2022), bbka.org.uk. National Bee Unit Beekeeper's Toolkit, nationalbeeunit.com. Snelgrove, L. E., Swarming: Its Control and Prevention (1934), standard UK reference for swarm-control principles still in wide use.

glossaryTerms.definition: A queen cell built by a colony preparing to swarm, typically found on the lower edge or face of a brood frame. The first capped swarm cell is the signal to act.
Forager-age bees old enough to have taken orientation flights and locked their home position. They return to the original hive stand regardless of where the hive body is moved.

content.text: When a colony reaches the edge of swarming, the beekeeper's job is to take control of the split before the bees do. The artificial swarm method works by exploiting the difference between flying bees and house bees: flying bees return to the original stand; house bees stay with the brood. The result is two colonies where there was one, with both ending up queenright by the end of the following week.
Open the hive when you find the first sealed
. If several cells are capped and the queen is gone, you have missed the primary swarm and the method changes (you are now managing a cast). Act on the first capped cell, not on cups or open larvae.

A full-size spare hive body (floor, brood box, crown board, roof) set on the original stand. The original hive will move to a new position at least a metre to the side. You need enough empty drawn comb to fill the new hive body, or foundation if drawn comb is not available. A spare queen excluder is useful but not essential.

Light the smoker and suit up. Smoke the entrance lightly, then lift the original hive off its stand and move it at least one metre to the side, ideally with the entrance pointing in a different direction to the original. Place the new empty hive body on the original stand with the entrance facing the same direction the bees know.

Work through the original hive on its new position. Find the queen. When you do, place the frame she is on into the new hive body on the original stand along with a frame of young open brood and two or three frames of drawn comb or foundation. Fill the remaining space with more drawn comb. Add a queen excluder between the floor and the brood box if you want to prevent her swarming before the colony has settled.
The original hive (now on the side position) keeps all the sealed brood, all the nurse bees, and the best

. Select one good cell and destroy all the others. Look for a cell that is plump, well-positioned on the lower face of the frame, and undamaged. If you cannot decide, leave two and remove the weaker after another four days.

come back from forage and return to the original stand. Because the new box is on that stand and smells of their queen and open brood, they enter it and boost the new colony substantially. The queen is now leading a large, young colony with no swarming urge. The old hive on the side raises a new queen from the selected cell.

Check the side hive after seven days to confirm the virgin queen is present and the remaining swarm cells are reduced to one. A week after that, check for eggs. If the colony has laying workers (scattered eggs in multiple cells per comb) rather than a mated queen, you will need to unite it back to the queenright colony through a sheet of newspaper.

attrs.cue: Both colonies are queenless after two weeks
attrs.cause: The selected swarm cell failed or the virgin queen was lost on her mating flight
attrs.fix: Introduce a frame of young eggs from the other colony; they will draw emergency queen cells. Or purchase a mated queen and introduce her in a push-in cage.

attrs.cue: The queenright colony issues a swarm within a week
attrs.cause: The queen excluder was not fitted and the colony still had swarm cells you missed
attrs.fix: Collect the swarm, cage it with its original queen, and use it to re-queen the side colony or start a nucleus. Check both hives thoroughly and reduce all cells except one.

attrs.cue: The flying bees unite into the wrong box
attrs.cause: Both boxes ended up facing the same way on similar stands
attrs.fix: Too late to change once the bees have orientated. Swap the hives' positions early the next morning before flying starts and let the bees re-orientate.

content.text: The artificial swarm is the most reliable swarm-control method for a small apiary. With one spare hive body and a bit of timing, a colony that was about to leave becomes two productive units by midsummer.
